title: Google to pay SpaceX $920M a month for Gemini compute capacity
subtitle: A 33-month contract sends roughly $30 billion to SpaceX in exchange for about 110,000 NVIDIA GPUs at xAI data centers.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: On June 5, 2026, Google disclosed it will pay SpaceX $920 million a month from October 2026 through June 2029 to lease compute at xAI data centers — roughly 110,000 NVIDIA GPUs plus CPUs, memory, and supporting hardware. The contract is worth about $30 billion over its lifetime. Google described the arrangement as a short-term bridge to meet higher-than-expected demand for its Gemini Enterprise platform. Capacity ramps up at a reduced fee through September, with the full monthly rate kicking in once Google takes delivery of the committed GPU pool.

The deal is the second in five weeks where SpaceX has signed a hyperscaler customer to a multibillion-dollar monthly compute lease. Anthropic agreed to pay $1.25 billion a month for Colossus capacity in May. Two of the three biggest U.S. AI labs are now renting infrastructure from a private space company that lives mostly outside the traditional cloud market — which says something about how thin frontier-scale compute supply has become. The 90-day termination clause that either party can invoke after December 31, 2026 is a tell that both sides consider this temporary scaffolding, not a permanent migration.

Twelve months ago, hyperscalers were the ones renting compute to AI startups. The flow has flipped: model labs and search companies are paying utility rates to anyone who can stand up GPUs at gigawatt scale. NVIDIA's GB200 supply, power siting, and grid interconnects are now the binding constraints on the whole sector. SpaceX has the spare cash from Starlink and the appetite to take three-year construction risk — and that combination has become a strategic AI position, almost by accident.

A note for learners: if you're tracking who wins the next phase of AI, watch power and floor space as carefully as model benchmarks. A lab's roadmap is only as fast as the data centers it can plug into, and the centers it can plug into are no longer all named AWS, Azure, or GCP. The bottleneck has moved from algorithms to amperage.

sources.title: Google will pay SpaceX $920M per month for compute — TechCrunch
